title: Blurb

jokes.text: Well, that just tickles my twat!
jokes.setup: A moving, feminist speech about Selina shattering the glass ceiling is read aloud — earnest, soaring rhetoric about her legacy for daughters everywhere.
jokes.punchline: Selina's private, visceral, vulgar response: 'Well, that just tickles my twat!' — completely undercutting the dignity of the moment with gleeful obscenity.
jokes.explanation: The gap between the lofty feminist rhetoric and Selina's crudely self-interested delight is enormous and perfectly timed. The vulgarity is character-specific — only Selina would phrase her satisfaction this way. The economy is exceptional: one line does all the work of establishing her as a narcissist who cares only how she sounds, not what she represents. Cold open joke that sets the episode's entire satirical register. Justifies the 9 because it's the perfect encapsulation of the show's thesis in one filthy line.

jokes.text: Gosh, Montez is actually gonna say that at my portrait unveiling in English?
jokes.setup: The speech quote is clearly in English. The question of language is irrelevant.
jokes.punchline: Selina asks if Montez will say it 'in English' — a casually racist/xenophobic aside about the Latina president, delivered as if it's a legitimate concern.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical character comedy — Selina's casual racism embedded in a compliment. Only works because of who Selina is and the show's willingness to let her be genuinely awful. The 'in English' assumption reveals her prejudice without any commentary needed.

jokes.text: See, Mike? That is good writing. Why can't you write like that?
jokes.setup: Selina praises the speech as good writing, then turns to Mike, her actual speechwriter.
jokes.punchline: She uses a speech written by someone else to insult Mike directly — 'Why can't you write like that?' — making him feel inadequate for not writing the speech he didn't write.
jokes.explanation: Standard Selina-belittles-Mike beat but executed with good economy. The comedic logic (criticizing Mike for not writing something he had no opportunity to write) is petty and precise. Reliable if not exceptional.

jokes.text: the White House wants us to finalize your guest list by the day after... yesterday. — So, today. That's a bit of a brain twister.
jokes.setup: Aide fumbles the phrasing of a deadline, meaning 'today' but saying 'day after yesterday.'
jokes.punchline: Selina cuts through it with withering deadpan: 'So, today. That's a bit of a brain twister.' — making the aide feel stupid for overcomplicating a simple concept.
jokes.explanation: Quick, dry wit. 'That's a bit of a brain twister' is the perfect condescending understatement. Competent Veep-style walk-and-talk burn that lands cleanly.

jokes.text: What about Congressman Jonah Ryan? — Richard, you have such a good sense of humor.
jokes.setup: Someone suggests Jonah Ryan for the guest list. There's a beat.
jokes.punchline: Selina assumes it must be a joke and compliments Richard for his sense of humor — the idea of inviting Jonah is so absurd to her that it couldn't be serious.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully structured two-beat joke. The setup of Jonah's name being floated, the implied pause/reaction, then Selina's pivot to treating it as comedy gold — this is efficient and character-perfect. Richard's deadpan relationship with Jonah makes this even richer. Justifies 8 through economy and perfect character integration: Selina can't conceive of Jonah being a legitimate guest.

jokes.text: Amy, you should take a cue from him, really.
jokes.setup: Selina has just praised Richard for his 'sense of humor' (i.e., suggesting Jonah). She pivots to telling Amy to be more like Richard.
jokes.punchline: Amy — a high-strung political operative — is told to take humor lessons from Richard, who has just accidentally made a serious suggestion. The insult is embedded and multi-layered.
jokes.explanation: Extension of the previous joke, adds a layer by transferring the insult to Amy. Competent but secondary — the first beat was stronger.

jokes.text: The book ends with you going off to college. — That's right, yes, and the presidential stuff will be in volume two... 'Meyer Ascendant.' — And three... 'The Meyer Year.'
jokes.setup: Editor tells Selina the memoir ends at college and needs more presidential content. Selina reveals she's planned it as a multi-volume epic.
jokes.punchline: 'Meyer Ascendant' and 'The Meyer Year' — grandiose, self-mythologizing titles for what is, at this point, a memoir about childhood and a horse.
jokes.explanation: Selina's delusional self-importance is perfectly expressed through the escalating trilogy titles. 'The Meyer Year' as a title for a single presidential term she barely mentions is peak Selina grandiosity. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: We're just gonna need our advance back. — There was an advance? — Quiet, quiet.
jokes.setup: The editor says if Selina keeps the book as-is, they need the advance back.
jokes.punchline: Amy's alarmed 'There was an advance?' — implying Selina spent/hid money from her own team. Selina's panicked 'Quiet, quiet' confirms the worst.
jokes.explanation: Three-beat joke perfectly compressed. Amy's genuine surprise reveals Selina's financial shadiness; Selina's 'Quiet, quiet' to her own aide in front of the editor is both funny and deeply revealing of her chaos. Excellent economy and structure.

jokes.text: And Moses led his people to the land of...? — Hanukkah.
jokes.setup: Rabbi asks Jonah, mid-conversion class, to complete the sentence about Moses.
jokes.punchline: Jonah confidently answers 'Hanukkah' — getting the most basic answer catastrophically wrong by substituting a holiday for a place name.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly pitched Jonah stupidity joke. 'Hanukkah' is the wrong category of answer entirely — he hasn't just gotten the answer wrong, he's gotten the type of answer wrong. The confidence implied is what makes it land. Clean structure.

jokes.text: Shut up, Nikhil. This stupid hat is too small for my head. — Yarmulke. — Fine, this stupid hat is too small for my yarmulke.
jokes.setup: Jonah complains about 'this stupid hat' being too small for his head. Someone corrects him: it's called a yarmulke.
jokes.punchline: Jonah accepts the correction but applies it nonsensically: 'this stupid hat is too small for my yarmulke' — as if 'yarmulke' is now the name of his head.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Jonah hears the correction and immediately applies it wrong — not just ignorant but wrong-shaped ignorant. The structure is almost syllogistic in its comedy: premise, correction, Jonah's absurd synthesis. 'Too small for my yarmulke' is highly quotable precisely because the logic is so broken yet internally consistent to Jonah. Justifies 8+ through originality of the comedic error type and the density of the laugh.

jokes.text: Oh, good, 'cause all this learning is giving me a yarmulke ache.
jokes.setup: The rabbi explains conversion is about commitment to Jewish lifestyle, not rote learning.
jokes.punchline: Jonah responds with 'yarmulke ache' — a pun on 'headache' using the word he just learned, proving he absorbed only the vocabulary, not the meaning.
jokes.explanation: Callback to the yarmulke correction that escalates it into a pun. 'Yarmulke ache' is the exact wrong application — he's using the word but substituting it for a body part concept again. Tight, quotable, and shows Jonah's brain can only use new words as blunt instruments.

jokes.text: Oh, Jesus Christ! — I'm sorry, I can say that now and still get into heaven, right? — Actually, Jonah, Jews don't believe in heaven. — Shut the fuck up, Nikhil!
jokes.setup: Jonah learns he's not on the White House unveiling list and exclaims 'Jesus Christ.' He immediately worries this violates his new religion.
jokes.punchline: When told Jews don't believe in heaven, Jonah's response ('Shut the fuck up, Nikhil!') reveals he only converted for the afterlife access — the entire premise of his conversion is shattered in one line.
jokes.explanation: Four-beat escalating joke that reveals Jonah's entire motivation for converting. The structure is excellent: the slip (Jesus Christ), the self-correction concern, the devastating information (no heaven), and the profane denial. Each beat tops the last. Justifies 8+ because it does meaningful character work (exposing Jonah's cynical self-interest) while being consistently funny.

jokes.text: Wait, Shawnee, how am I gonna see my dog again? Is there, like, a Jew place for dogs?
jokes.setup: Jonah has just been told Jews don't believe in heaven. He's now processing what this means for his afterlife.
jokes.punchline: His primary concern: seeing his dead dog. His solution-seeking: asking if there's 'a Jew place for dogs.' The theological anxiety is entirely about pets.
jokes.explanation: The 'Jew place for dogs' phrasing is delightful in its ignorance — Jonah genuinely trying to navigate theology with zero framework. The absurdity of the concern (the dog, not spiritual growth) is perfectly Jonah.

jokes.text: We're fucked. — I don't even remember any of that. — Some things are so dull, you have to block them out.
jokes.setup: Mike reads from his diary — a packed day of government meetings and briefings — hoping it will help Selina write her memoirs.
jokes.punchline: Amy's 'We're fucked,' then Selina's confession she doesn't remember any of it, then Mike's defensive 'Some things are so dull, you have to block them out' — all three characters reveal the same truth from different angles.
jokes.explanation: A triple-beat that works through accumulation. Each character's response reveals they all checked out during the presidency — a great satirical point about how mundane actual governance is. Well-structured but not exceptional.

jokes.text: I mean, I'm sure that I played with Catherine when she was a child... but for the life of me, I can't remember one second.
jokes.setup: Selina is lamenting her inability to remember her presidency for the memoir.
jokes.punchline: She extends the memory failure to her own child — she 'assumes' she played with Catherine but has no actual memory of it. Delivered as a mild inconvenience.
jokes.explanation: Quietly devastating. Selina's casual admission that she has no memory of her own child's childhood, treated as a minor footnote to her memoir problems, is dark and perfectly in character. Justifies the high character integration score — this is entirely specific to Selina.

jokes.text: So, is it a boy, is it a girl? — Oh, right. — It's a baby.
jokes.setup: Catherine announces they had a wellness check. Selina eagerly asks the sex of the baby.
jokes.punchline: After a pause, someone deadpans 'It's a baby' — the most obvious possible answer to a question about a pregnancy.
jokes.explanation: Dry and economical. The non-answer as an answer. Works as a setup for the bigger gender-politics exchange that follows.

jokes.text: Catherine, you are not going to be coy or twee about the sex of my grandchild. This is a human being you're making, not some Brooklyn-based, artisanal chocolate bar.
jokes.setup: Catherine refuses to reveal the baby's sex, being deliberately mysterious about it.
jokes.punchline: 'Not some Brooklyn-based, artisanal chocolate bar' — the specific cultural reference as an insult, equating trendy gender-reveal evasiveness with hipster commodity culture.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'Brooklyn-based, artisanal chocolate bar' is a beautifully specific, culturally devastating simile. It's funny as a standalone observation but also perfectly captures Selina's contempt for pretension. The word 'artisanal' carries enormous comedic weight here. Highly quotable and original. Justifies 8+ through specificity and quotability.

jokes.text: Birth gender isn't even that relevant. She/he will decide her/his ideal gender when she/he is/are ready.
jokes.setup: The question of the baby's sex hangs in the air. Marjorie offers the progressive perspective.
jokes.punchline: The pronoun construction 'she/he is/are ready' escalates into grammatical absurdity — trying to be inclusive but producing a sentence that no one can parse.
jokes.explanation: The 'is/are' construction is the comedic peak — the sentence grammatically collapses under its own inclusivity. Marjorie's robot-deadpan delivery of increasingly broken language is consistent with her character.

jokes.text: Okay, is that how we're talking now, Marjorie, like some sort of bi-curious Porky Pig?
jokes.setup: Marjorie has just delivered the pronoun-stacked sentence about gender identity.
jokes.punchline: 'Bi-curious Porky Pig' — the pronoun stuttering likened to Porky Pig's stutter, with 'bi-curious' as a layered sexual orientation jab at the pronoun ambiguity.
jokes.explanation: One of the funniest lines in the episode. The 'Porky Pig' comparison is inspired — pronoun stuttering literally sounds like Porky's speech pattern, and 'bi-curious' layers in a sexual orientation dimension that makes it exponentially funnier. The compound insult is dense with meaning, sounds amazing out loud, and is wholly original. Justifies 8.5+ through originality, quotability, and the elegance of the connection between two seemingly unrelated concepts.

jokes.text: I had an aunt who transitioned twice. She was trapped inside a man, and then that man was trapped inside of another woman. — Oh, like a turducken.
jokes.setup: Marjorie shares a personal family story about gender transition to illustrate her point.
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'Oh, like a turducken' — reducing a complex human experience to a nested poultry cooking metaphor.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional joke. The turducken comparison — a turkey stuffed with a duck stuffed with a chicken — is structurally perfect as a metaphor for nested gender transitions. Selina applies it with such earnest confidence, as if she's been helpful. The word 'turducken' alone is funny; as a reductive metaphor for gender identity, it becomes a comedic gem. Highly quotable. Justifies 8+ because the connection is genuinely surprising and the economy is flawless.

jokes.text: Yes, Leslie or Dana if it's a boy and... And for a girl we like Linus. — Seriously? — That feels like child abuse.
jokes.setup: Catherine reveals the baby names they've chosen — gender-neutral names assigned in a deliberately inverted way.
jokes.punchline: Gendered boy names for a girl and 'Linus' (associated with the Peanuts boy character) for a girl. Selina's 'That feels like child abuse' lands the audience's exact response.
jokes.explanation: Solid joke — the inverted gender names punchline works, and 'that feels like child abuse' is Selina voicing audience sentiment. Competent execution of a well-structured beat.

jokes.text: Richard has doula class and we're bringing the snacks. — What? — It'll be my first human birth, so.
jokes.setup: Catherine mentions Richard (Selina's aide) is involved in their birth plans.
jokes.punchline: 'It'll be my first human birth' — implies Richard has attended non-human births. Left entirely unexplained, which makes it funnier.
jokes.explanation: Richard's 'It'll be my first human birth' is one of the episode's best lines. The implication that he's attended animal births is left completely unaddressed, which is exactly the right choice. Maximum funny per word. Deeply character-specific — only Richard would say this with complete earnestness. Justifies 8.5 through exceptional economy and character integration.

jokes.text: Gender is simple! Right. You're a girl, I'm a boy. Well. Hey, what about some of that lezzy stuff?
jokes.setup: Selina is exasperated by the gender conversation and declares it simple. Then immediately pivots to asking Gary about 'lezzy stuff' for the book.
jokes.punchline: The hard pivot from 'gender is simple' to 'what about some of that lezzy stuff' — Selina's hypocrisy is immediate and self-aware-free.
jokes.explanation: The whiplash from Selina's 'gender is simple' proclamation to her immediate exploitation of gay content for book sales is classic Selina — performative conservatism masking total cynicism. Good structure and character integration.

jokes.text: Yeah, but, I mean, does anybody really give a shit about two average-looking lesbians?
jokes.setup: Gary floats the idea of a Catherine/Secret Service affair for the book.
jokes.punchline: Selina dismisses it on the grounds that the lesbians aren't attractive enough to sell books — a triple insult (to Catherine, to the agent, and to gay representation in general).
jokes.explanation: Selina's calculus is purely commercial — not homophobic exactly, just savagely indifferent to anything that doesn't serve her. The specificity of 'average-looking' makes it cutting and funny.

jokes.text: A book about my sex life... People would pay whatever it is a book costs, you know?
jokes.setup: Selina floats the idea of a tell-all sex book.
jokes.punchline: 'People would pay whatever it is a book costs' — Selina clearly has no idea what books cost, her detachment from ordinary life revealed in one throwaway clause.
jokes.explanation: The aside 'whatever it is a book costs' is the surprise — in the middle of a grand proclamation, Selina reveals she doesn't know what retail prices are. Exquisite character detail.

jokes.text: All due respect, ma'am, we all knew you were, uh... ...with Kent.
jokes.setup: Selina hints she slept with someone she worked with. Mike assumes it's Kent.
jokes.punchline: The idea that Selina slept with Kent — the robotic data analyst — is so absurd that it recontextualizes both characters hilariously.
jokes.explanation: The misdirect works because Kent is the last person anyone would guess. The 'everyone knew' framing makes it worse — they apparently all just assumed this was happening. Good setup for the actual revelation.

jokes.text: My snatch isn't a data port!
jokes.setup: Mike has just suggested everyone assumed Selina was sleeping with Kent, the data-obsessed analyst.
jokes.punchline: 'My snatch isn't a data port!' — Selina's horrified rebuttal uses Kent's defining characteristic (data/technology) to reject the notion sexually.
jokes.explanation: The best line in the episode. Six words that: (1) are maximally vulgar in a precise way, (2) use Kent's defining characteristic (data) as the insult vehicle, (3) are completely character-specific to Selina, (4) would not work in any other show, (5) are instantly quotable. This is Veep writing at its apex. Competes with the all-time anchor examples. The specificity of 'data port' — not just 'computer' — is what elevates it. Justifies 9+ because it is truly exceptional in economy and character integration.

jokes.text: I slept with Tom James! — Whoa! — No! — In the white-hot White House.
jokes.setup: After rejecting the Kent assumption, Selina reveals the actual person.
jokes.punchline: 'In the white-hot White House' — the alliterative, self-dramatizing phrase she's apparently prepared for this revelation, as if she's been rehearsing the announcement.
jokes.explanation: The reactions (Whoa! No!) are part of the joke. 'In the white-hot White House' is Selina's own romanticized framing — she can't resist dramatizing even a confession. The alliteration feels like she workshopped it.

jokes.text: On a couch. — Ma'am, we have a best-seller! — I mean, it was the Green Room, but. — What? How do you know that? — You... you said it was at the Congressional Ball.
jokes.setup: Selina confirms the Tom James affair. Mike immediately sees dollar signs. Selina corrects the location.
jokes.punchline: Amy reveals she already knew — and has been fact-checking Selina's previous account. The layers of prior disclosure are revealed: Selina already told people, already got the details wrong, and doesn't remember what she said.
jokes.explanation: The escalating revelation that Amy already knew, already has prior testimony from Selina, and Selina can't keep her own stories straight — this is dense with comedic information and pays off in multiple directions. Great structure.

jokes.text: Or I could go and I could see him in person. — No, no, no, no, no, no. — You want me to go with? I can take notes. — Yes, yes, yes, yes, yes. — No. Mm-mm.
jokes.setup: Selina proposes visiting Tom James in person about the book affair revelation.
jokes.punchline: Amy's rapid-fire 'No' vs. Gary's immediate 'Yes, yes, yes' — the two aides in complete opposition, with Gary's eagerness to join and take notes being the absurd detail.
jokes.explanation: The comic contrast between Amy's horror and Gary's enthusiasm is reliable Veep machinery. 'I can take notes' is the best specific detail — Gary would absolutely want to document this.

jokes.text: God, how can anybody jerk off to her with him sitting right there?
jokes.setup: Jonah watches the CBS morning show with Dan and Brie co-anchoring.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's primary issue with the new co-anchor pairing is that it ruins his ability to masturbate to the female anchor — the most Jonah possible response to a journalism discussion.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Jonah. His concerns are always base and self-interested. The framing of the co-anchor problem as a masturbation logistics issue is grotesquely on-brand. Character integration is near-perfect.

jokes.text: I'm sure you'll find a way, Congressman.
jokes.setup: Jonah has just said it's hard to masturbate to the female anchor with Dan sitting there.
jokes.punchline: An aide responds with complete deadpan: 'I'm sure you'll find a way, Congressman.' — acknowledging the problem earnestly while implicitly calling Jonah a disgusting creep.
jokes.explanation: The aide's confidence in Jonah's depravity, delivered without judgment, is a great deadpan beat. 'I'm sure you'll find a way' functions both as encouragement and as a devastating character assessment.

jokes.text: Because we worked for the president and didn't cost her the presidency. And because we don't look like Herman Munster's brother who liked to molest that pudgy werewolf kid.
jokes.setup: Jonah asks why Dan and Brie got invited to the Meyer unveiling when he didn't.
jokes.punchline: Dan's two-part answer: the professional reason, then the physical/character assassination — 'Herman Munster's brother who liked to molest that pudgy werewolf kid.'
jokes.explanation: The insult is specific and image-rich — comparing Jonah to a fictional sibling of a famous monster who has an even worse crime. The 'pudgy werewolf kid' specification is an excellent absurdist extension. Insult comedy at a high level.

jokes.text: Charlie, also played by Fred Gwynne. Season one, episode nine.
jokes.setup: Dan has just compared Jonah to Herman Munster's brother Charlie.
jokes.punchline: Kent immediately provides the reference: 'Charlie, also played by Fred Gwynne. Season one, episode nine.' — as if this is the most important clarification to make.
jokes.explanation: Kent's pedantic data-correction of a casual insult is transcendently in character. He's not responding to the spirit of the insult — he's fact-checking the citation. 'Season one, episode nine' as the comedically specific detail is perfect. This is the Kent joke — only he would do this. Justifies 8.8 through exceptional character integration and the surprise of the pedantic redirect.

jokes.text: Kent, can I have your plus-one? — No, I'm bringing my jai alai instructor. — Female. — Ish.
jokes.setup: Jonah asks for Kent's plus-one to the unveiling. Kent says he has a date.
jokes.punchline: The 'Female. — Ish.' exchange — Kent's jai alai instructor's gender is ambiguous, and Kent indicates this with minimal words. The casualness of 'ish' is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Three syllables of dialogue do enormous work. 'Ish' as Kent's non-elaboration is perfectly economical — he provides no context, no judgment, just data. The word lands like a bomb because of what it implies and what it leaves out. Justifies 8+ through extraordinary economy.

jokes.text: What about your plus-one? — I work for you, I don't date you. — Maybe I'm gonna go hire some women, and I'm gonna make them date me.
jokes.setup: Jonah is denied the plus-one and asks his own aide.
jokes.punchline: 'Maybe I'm gonna go hire some women, and I'm gonna make them date me.' — Jonah's solution to his dating life is to weaponize employment. The casual threat/plan is deeply disturbing and funny.
jokes.explanation: Reveals Jonah's complete inability to understand social dynamics — he would literally pay for companionship through employment contracts. Dark and on-brand.

jokes.text: And tomorrow, a sex offender registry for dogs.
jokes.setup: The CBS morning show teases its upcoming segments.
jokes.punchline: A 'sex offender registry for dogs' as a news segment — absurdly specific, treated with complete broadcast-news seriousness.
jokes.explanation: The specific absurdity of a 'sex offender registry for dogs' treated as routine morning-show content is very funny. Economy is near-perfect — five words that suggest an entire absurd universe.

jokes.text: It's about time. - Mm-hmm.
jokes.setup: Dan announces a sex offender registry for dogs.
jokes.punchline: Both anchors respond with earnest agreement ('It's about time / Mm-hmm') — treating it as legitimate public policy progress.
jokes.explanation: The button to joke #34. Two syllables of sincere agreement is the right button. Works as a reaction beat and callback together.

jokes.text: What'd you think of that new sign-off? — You sound like a Scientologist.
jokes.setup: Dan is proud of his new sign-off ('every day starts with morning').
jokes.punchline: 'You sound like a Scientologist.' — precise, cutting, and true: the sign-off does have that positive-affirmation, cult-adjacent energy.
jokes.explanation: Clean and well-observed. The Scientology comparison is unexpected but immediately recognizable once stated — that's good comedic writing. Strong economy.

jokes.text: People like the energy that you and Jane had. It was this raw, sexual vibe because you two were... — Jesus, we were not fucking! — Okay, going down on each other, whatever. I don't wanna know!
jokes.setup: The producer tries to diplomatically describe the chemistry Dan and Jane had on air.
jokes.punchline: Dan's horrified denial triggers the producer to escalate to 'going down on each other, whatever' — revealing they absolutely believe it and have moved past caring.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'sexual vibe' to 'going down on each other, whatever' is good — the producer's breezy indifference to the specifics ('whatever') is funny. Good structure and rhythm.

jokes.text: But I... I'm actually fucking her. — Dan! — Aren't you engaged to a Yankee? — Yeah, I am. — That's why I told you not to tell anybody. — Aw, that's sweet.
jokes.setup: Dan tries to prove he and Brie have chemistry by revealing they're actually sleeping together.
jokes.punchline: The reveal that he's cheating on his fiancée, Brie knew this was secret, and the producer responds to the affair news with 'Aw, that's sweet.' — the mismatch of registers is the joke.
jokes.explanation: 'Aw, that's sweet' as the response to an affair confession is the best moment here. The escalating revelations (engagement, secrecy, cheating) are fast and well-paced.

jokes.text: Maybe you two should fuck differently, then. I don't know. — That's where I draw the line, man. I don't take notes on how I fuck.
jokes.setup: The producer suggests Dan and Brie improve their on-screen chemistry.
jokes.punchline: 'Maybe you two should fuck differently' as broadcast-network professional advice. Dan draws a principled line — he'll do anything for the show except change his sexual technique.
jokes.explanation: 'I don't take notes on how I fuck' is Dan's principled stand — in a show where he takes notes on everything professionally, his refusal here is character-revealing and funny. Good integration.

jokes.text: I, however... I am willing to do anything, because I cannot lose this job, not before my wedding!
jokes.setup: Dan refuses to change his sexual approach for the job. Brie immediately offers the opposite.
jokes.punchline: Brie will literally do anything — presumably including fucking differently — because she needs the job. The wedding as the deadline is a specific and sad detail.
jokes.explanation: Brie's desperation contrasting with Dan's casual confidence is a solid running character beat. The wedding timeline adds pathos-comedy.

jokes.text: Um, also... just a little thing here... people don't like you in yellow. — Okay. — See? Told you.
jokes.setup: After serious professional feedback about on-air chemistry, the producer adds one more note.
jokes.punchline: 'People don't like you in yellow' — buried as an afterthought, delivered identically to the previous serious feedback. Dan's 'Told you' reveals he's been saying this for a while.
jokes.explanation: The mundane costume note dropped after existential career advice is a good tonal contrast. Dan's prior warning adds a layer — he's been right about something domestic while being wrong about everything important.

jokes.text: Yes, I wrote a blurb for your upcoming tome. — Of course you did. — Sight unseen.
jokes.setup: Tom James casually mentions he wrote a blurb for Selina's book.
jokes.punchline: 'Sight unseen' — he blurbed a book he's never read, which is both ridiculously sycophantic and reveals the meaninglessness of blurbs as a form.
jokes.explanation: Good economy — 'sight unseen' lands as a two-word punchline that does the work. Sets up the episode's title payoff (blurb) and is characteristically Washington in its political back-scratching.

jokes.text: Yes, what is... the title is 'Investing with a Conscience'? — 'Conscience,' yes. — So, it's sci-fi?
jokes.setup: Tom James mentions his upcoming book about ethical investing.
jokes.punchline: 'So, it's sci-fi?' — Selina immediately categorizes a book about investing with conscience as fantasy, implying conscience doesn't exist in their world.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's sharpest lines. The genre reclassification is instant and devastating — 'conscience' in Washington is literally as fictional as science fiction. The joke works on multiple levels: as a burn on Tom James, as a satire of DC cynicism, and as pure comedic economy. Justifies 8.5+ through originality of the comedic concept and near-perfect structure.

jokes.text: I guess my ego allowed me to believe that folksy, old Tom James was above all that beltway horseshit. And in the end, I just turned out to be another blue bottle fly feeding on a heaping mouthful of Seabiscuit's finest.
jokes.setup: Tom James delivers a sincere-seeming apology to Selina for betraying her.
jokes.punchline: The metaphor escalates into something genuinely baffling — 'blue bottle fly feeding on a heaping mouthful of Seabiscuit's finest.' The folksy wisdom collapses into horse-manure imagery.
jokes.explanation: Tom James's apology collapses into a weirdly specific horse-dung metaphor that undermines its own sincerity. 'Seabiscuit's finest' is gloriously specific — not just horse manure but the horse manure of a famous racehorse. The pomposity of the folksy register crashing into feces imagery is inspired. Justifies 8+ through originality and the sustained comedy of watching a sophisticated man say something this absurd with full commitment.

jokes.text: Wow! [beat — Selina's reaction to the Seabiscuit metaphor]
jokes.setup: Tom James has just ended his apology with the Seabiscuit horse-manure metaphor.
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'Wow!' — a single word that perfectly captures the audience's confusion and the absurdity of the metaphor, neither accepting nor rejecting the apology.
jokes.explanation: The single word doing maximum comedic work. 'Wow' is the only appropriate response to the Seabiscuit speech, and Selina's delivery (clearly confused but trying to be gracious) adds a layer.

jokes.text: If it makes you feel any better, if I was in your shoes, I probably would've tried to steal the presidency, too. — Well, that's what made the two of us such a beatable combination.
jokes.setup: Selina tries to make Tom feel better about his betrayal by normalizing it.
jokes.punchline: Tom's comeback — 'that's what made the two of us such a beatable combination' — turns her generosity into a shared indictment of both their characters.
jokes.explanation: Sharp and structurally elegant. Selina's 'consolation' is accidentally self-damning, and Tom's pivot turns it into a mutual condemnation. The political double meaning (beatable as a ticket AND mutually destructive as people) is well-executed.

jokes.text: Well, I guess I will have to think outside my box.
jokes.setup: Tom has begged Selina not to include the affair in her book. She's forced to reconsider her memoir strategy.
jokes.punchline: 'Think outside my box' — an inadvertent double entendre, with 'box' as slang for vagina, given the entire conversation has been about their sexual encounter.
jokes.explanation: The double entendre lands because of its context — Selina probably doesn't intend it but she might. The ambiguity is part of the joke. Solid wordplay, well-placed.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, why did I have to fuck the last remaining gentleman in Washington? — God! — Who said that? Was that Dolley Madison? — It was James Madison.
jokes.setup: Selina is frustrated that Tom's gentlemanliness prevents her from using the affair in the book.
jokes.punchline: Gary's attempt to attribute the quote ('Was that Dolley Madison?') is wrong; Mike corrects him ('It was James Madison') — but nobody addresses that this is not a Madison quote. The correction is irrelevant either way.
jokes.explanation: Two layers of comedy: Selina's self-pitying 'last remaining gentleman in Washington,' then Gary's absurd attempt to source the quote to a First Lady, then Mike's pedantic correction of the wrong attribution — all while missing that nobody said this. The cascade of stupidity escalating from a genuine moment of pathos is very Veep.

jokes.text: Okay, Mike, get up. — So, how'd it go? — Well, he just begged me not to put it in. — So, none of it goes in the book? — No, it all goes in.
jokes.setup: Mike has been apparently waiting outside. Selina reports back on the Tom James meeting.
jokes.punchline: Selina reveals that Tom's begging has had zero effect — 'it all goes in.' His gentleman's plea was utterly wasted.
jokes.explanation: The 'it all goes in' reversal is satisfying and characteristically Selina. The double entendre with 'put it in' / 'it all goes in' continues the sexual wordplay thread.

jokes.text: Hey, did you get invited to the Meyer unveiling? — Everyone was unless you're a mole person who was cast out of his underground society for keistering sewer rats.
jokes.setup: Someone asks Furlong about the unveiling invitation.
jokes.punchline: 'Mole person who was cast out of his underground society for keistering sewer rats' — an impossibly specific and disgusting image for why someone might not be invited, clearly aimed at Jonah.
jokes.explanation: Furlong's insult vocabulary is the show's gold standard, and 'keistering sewer rats' as the crime that gets you expelled from mole society is peak grotesque specificity. The escalating image (mole person → underground society → expelled for sexual acts with rodents) is almost too much, which is exactly right. Justifies 8+ through originality and the sheer density of the image.

jokes.text: You are addicted to spending like the proverbial drunken sailor.
jokes.setup: Furlong is addressing a political opponent's spending habits in a meeting.
jokes.punchline: 'The proverbial drunken sailor' — using 'proverbial' to lend gravitas to an old cliché, which is itself a satirical comment on political rhetoric.
jokes.explanation: A weaker Furlong moment — the joke is more about the absurdity of Furlong using 'proverbial' in a cliché than anything particularly fresh. Competent but well below his usual standard.

jokes.text: Well, it's your casa white-o, as you would say.
jokes.setup: Jonah is told the invitation is President Meyer's 'fiesta, as you would say' — a patronizing remark directed at President Montez.
jokes.punchline: Jonah fires back with 'your casa white-o' — combining 'White House' with Spanish-ish phrasing to mock Montez, further revealing his ignorance and casual racism.
jokes.explanation: The 'casa white-o' construction is a clever portmanteau insult — Jonah's racism is clumsy but almost creative here. The callback to the 'fiesta, as you would say' setup earns the punchline.

jokes.text: You haven't heard the last of Jonah Ryan. — Okay, Jeffersons, sit down. The Black Caucus gets this room back in 20 minutes. I do not wanna piss those guys off.
jokes.setup: Jonah makes a dramatic declaration as he exits the meeting.
jokes.punchline: He immediately undercuts it by being cowed by the Black Caucus scheduling conflict — his threat is deflated in the same breath.
jokes.explanation: The self-deflation is classic Jonah — his dramatic moments are always immediately undercut by his cowardice or stupidity. The Black Caucus detail is a good specific.

jokes.text: Cutting aid to Israel! — Whoa, no! We're not gonna do that. Are you crazy? — Cutting aid to veterans. — Great! — They volunteered, right?
jokes.setup: The Jeffersons are brainstorming bold political moves to get attention.
jokes.punchline: Israel is immediately vetoed; veterans' aid is immediately accepted. 'They volunteered, right?' is the morally bankrupt rationale delivered as if it settles the matter.
jokes.explanation: 'They volunteered, right?' as the moral logic for cutting veterans' benefits is sharp political satire — the cruelty presented as a reasonable conclusion from a technical premise. Good economy.

jokes.text: Why do you keep saying Tom James and I made love? Are you a 15-year-old girl?
jokes.setup: Mike is apparently using 'made love' as the phrasing for the Selina/Tom James encounter in his notes.
jokes.punchline: Selina objects to the romantic euphemism, equating 'made love' with the vocabulary of teenage girls — which also tells us something about what kind of language she prefers.
jokes.explanation: Selina's preference for coarser vocabulary over romantic euphemism is consistent and funny. Mike's oblivious use of 'made love' is perfectly in character for him.

jokes.text: No, see, you gotta find a different word than 'screwed.' It's just hard to come up with so many different ways to curse.
jokes.setup: Selina is directing Mike on memoir language.
jokes.punchline: 'It's just hard to come up with so many different ways to curse' — Mike's complaint that vulgarity is actually challenging creative work. Delivered with total earnestness.
jokes.explanation: Mike's writer's-block complaint being about profanity diversity is a great character beat — he's treating vulgarity as a craft challenge, which is both true and absurd.

jokes.text: Okay, so I have got a White House book that is hotter than Nancy Reagan's guide to cocksucking.
jokes.setup: Selina calls her editor Greg to pitch the revised book concept.
jokes.punchline: 'Hotter than Nancy Reagan's guide to cocksucking' — a gloriously specific and outrageous celebrity historical comparison that treats an alleged piece of First Lady mythology as a reference point.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Nancy Reagan' and the specificity of the sexual act referenced creates an extremely specific, audacious comparison. The joke treats this as a known benchmark ('hotter than...') which elevates the absurdity. Highly quotable and a great example of Veep's willingness to go all the way. Justifies 8+ through originality and the sheer audacity of the reference.

jokes.text: Richard, go and turn on CBS right now. — Hey, Tom James is on TV. — He looks good.
jokes.setup: Selina cuts off the editor call. Tom James is apparently on CBS.
jokes.punchline: Gary's immediate aesthetic assessment of Tom James — 'He looks good' — is both irrelevant and totally Gary.
jokes.explanation: Gary's gay-coded fandom of politically relevant men is a consistent character joke. Works in context but not exceptional.

jokes.text: That's the reason I've always said that Selina Meyer's political acumen is sharp as any you'll find in Washington. — ...I did not expect to find myself on a couch in the Green Room at the White House, um... — Having sex with President Selina Meyer.
jokes.setup: Tom James seems to be giving a complimentary interview about Selina. Dan fills in the blank.
jokes.punchline: Dan completes 'Having sex with President Selina Meyer' as if finishing Tom's sentence — the reveal that this is the context of the interview is immediate and devastating.
jokes.explanation: The structure here is impeccable — the compliment sets up the expectation of a normal interview, the pause after 'um...' builds anticipation, and Dan's completion of the sentence as if it's obvious creates the punchline. The transition from praise to 'having sex with' is a masterclass in misdirection. Justifies 8+ through structure and the clean subversion of expectation.

jokes.text: 'Vanity Fair?' Mike, how did we not know about this? — They called me a couple months ago and I thought they were trying to get me to renew my prescription.
jokes.setup: Tom James's affair story has apparently been excerpted in Vanity Fair. Selina demands to know why they didn't know.
jokes.punchline: Mike heard 'Vanity Fair' and thought they were calling about a medical prescription — confusing the magazine with a pharmacy.
jokes.explanation: Mike's incompetence reaching new heights — he can't distinguish between a prestigious magazine and a pharmacy call. 'Prescription' vs 'subscription' is a simple confusion but the specific failure mode is perfectly Mike: overwhelmed, medicated, out of the loop. The correction — 'Subscription!' — is excellent timing.

jokes.text: She's a year older than me. — Okay, that is a fucking lie! — I mean, where's the follow-up question? — This is disinformation!
jokes.setup: Tom James describes Selina as 'something of a mentor' on TV.
jokes.punchline: The 'mentor' framing is betrayal enough, but 'she's a year older than me' revealing that she's barely older triggers Selina's outrage at the implication she's aged/senior to him. 'Disinformation' being applied to a one-year age gap is the escalation.
jokes.explanation: Selina's vanity about being called a mentor/older is characteristically petty. 'This is disinformation!' applied to something technically true but cosmetically insulting is good satirical commentary on her relationship with truth.

jokes.text: Now, Senator, was President Meyer the aggressor? — Your word, not mine. — But again, yes. — I didn't rape him! — He wishes!
jokes.setup: The TV interview asks if Selina was 'the aggressor' in the encounter.
jokes.punchline: Tom carefully says 'Your word, not mine. But again, yes.' Then Selina's 'I didn't rape him!' is immediately topped by Gary's deeply weird 'He wishes!' — implying Tom would have wanted to be assaulted by Selina.
jokes.explanation: Four-beat escalation that's near-perfect. Tom's lawyerly 'your word, not mine. But again, yes' is devastating. Selina's horrified denial raises the stakes. Then Gary's 'He wishes!' — delivered presumably with total Gary-loyalty earnestness — is the perfect comedic punctuation that crosses a line in the most hilarious way. The structure builds perfectly to that final beat. Justifies 8.7 through structural excellence and Gary's character-perfect absurdity.

jokes.text: I can't believe I said that steaming turd of a book was a refreshing take on Wall Street aimed at your conscience and your funny bone. — Hey, you used my blurb! — Oh, quiet up, Mike.
jokes.setup: Selina is watching Tom James promote 'Investing with a Conscience' on TV.
jokes.punchline: Mike reveals his blurb was used in the book — pride in his minor contribution while Selina's use of 'steaming turd' to describe a book she blurbed is self-incriminating. 'Quiet up' is a nice Mike-ism.
jokes.explanation: Pays off the blurb exchange from the Tom James meeting. Mike's pride in the blurb is absurd given the context. Selina's self-description of her own endorsement as a 'steaming turd' reveals she knew and didn't care.

jokes.text: I took Andrew's infidelity, Catherine's Catherine-ness, okay? But this I cannot take.
jokes.setup: Selina is listing the indignities she's endured.
jokes.punchline: 'Catherine's Catherine-ness' — reducing her daughter to an essence that requires no elaboration, implying Catherine's existence is an ongoing tribulation.
jokes.explanation: 'Catherine's Catherine-ness' is one of the episode's best character comedy moments — the nominalization of a person as their own defining burden is deeply economical and character-perfect. Only Selina would refer to her daughter this way, and the audience understands exactly what it means without any elaboration. Justifies 8+ through exceptional economy and character integration.

jokes.text: This is like Black History Month. It never fucking ends!
jokes.setup: Selina is in despair about the Tom James situation continuing.
jokes.punchline: Comparing her Tom James problem to Black History Month — an annual observance — as something that 'never fucking ends,' a comment simultaneously about her exasperation and her casual racism.
jokes.explanation: Dark and character-specific — Selina's casual invocation of a culturally significant observance to describe her personal grievance reveals her self-centered worldview. The 'it never fucking ends' applied to a month-long observance is the comedic engine.

jokes.text: Do you want me to 69 him? — Oh, 86. — Yeah.
jokes.setup: Selina needs Tom James removed from some plan. Gary offers to help.
jokes.punchline: Gary says '69' when he means '86' (eliminate/cancel). The number confusion is its own joke; Selina corrects to '86,' Amy confirms. The sexualized number Gary chose is inadvertent.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy — three words ('Do you want me to 69 him?') generate enormous comedy from the wrong number, the sexual connotation, and Gary's obliviousness. The correction and confirmation are perfectly quick. A great Gary moment. Justifies 8+ through economy and the density of comedy in so few words.

jokes.text: Nobody since Jackie has given as much for their country as you.
jokes.setup: Gary is trying to motivate Selina to attend the portrait unveiling.
jokes.punchline: Gary's genuinely-meant comparison of Selina to Jackie Kennedy — as if Selina's suffering is equivalent to witnessing her husband's assassination — delivered in total earnest.
jokes.explanation: Gary's blind devotion producing hyperbolic equivalences is consistent character comedy. The Jackie comparison is absurdly elevated but Gary means every word.

jokes.text: If Jackie Kennedy can go through the day with her husband's philandering brains all over her dress and her head held high... even though they made her ride around in a convertible like an animal, I might add...
jokes.setup: Gary is invoking Jackie Kennedy as a model of dignified suffering.
jokes.punchline: He calls JFK's assassination 'philandering brains' — combining his infidelity with his death in a grotesque portmanteau — then adds the convertible complaint as an additional grievance on Jackie's behalf.
jokes.explanation: 'Her husband's philandering brains' is one of the most inspired lines in the episode — collapsing JFK's infidelity and his assassination into a single grotesque image. The follow-up complaint about the convertible ('like an animal') is the perfect absurdist escalation — as if the manner of travel was the additional indignity. Justifies 9 through genuine originality of the comedic concept and the audacity of the execution.

jokes.text: you can go to the White House tomorrow and forget all about those 15 minutes where he couldn't even unzip the back of your dress. — Wait a minute, what? How do you know that? — It was in Mike's notes.
jokes.setup: Gary reveals an extremely specific detail about Selina's sexual encounter with Tom James.
jokes.punchline: Selina's horrified 'How do you know that?' and Gary's matter-of-fact 'It was in Mike's notes' — both the specificity of the detail (couldn't unzip the dress) and the revelation that Mike catalogued it.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of the detail (couldn't unzip the dress) is humiliating and specific; the explanation that Mike documented this in his notes is both hilarious and appalling. The 'Oh, right' from Selina — accepting that Mike would have noted this — is a great button. Strong character integration across all three beats.

jokes.text: I'm not gonna have a good time. And if one person mentions Tom James to me, I'm gonna go piss in that punch bowl.
jokes.setup: Selina agrees, reluctantly, to attend the unveiling.
jokes.punchline: Her conditions for attendance: she reserves the right to urinate in the punch bowl if provoked. Delivered as a reasonable negotiating position.
jokes.explanation: The punch bowl threat is both absurd and completely plausible for Selina. The framing as a conditional ('if one person mentions Tom James') treats it as a proportionate response. Sets up a callback potential that pays off later.

jokes.text: Well, well, well, sorry, boys, no votes today on legalizing tiny Fleshlights for micro wangs. — Except for you, Gellardi. I'd wager it takes a whole roll of duct tape to strap down your hog of a cock.
jokes.setup: Furlong interrupts a vote, insulting the entire room.
jokes.punchline: The juxtaposition of insulting everyone for tiny penises, then immediately reversing to compliment Gellardi for having an enormous one — the whiplash is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Furlong's insult comedy at peak Veep level. 'Tiny Fleshlights for micro wangs' is vivid; the pivot to 'hog of a cock' requiring duct tape is the escalation that pays it off. The specificity of the duct tape is the detail that makes it great. Justifies 8+ through the structural whiplash and the sheer vividness of the imagery.

jokes.text: Now the clots in his legs are gonna rush to his brain. And maybe you were too busy biting James Bond's cable car wire to realize...
jokes.setup: Furlong is furious that Jonah's group has disrupted the debt ceiling vote, forcing Speaker Marwood to rush over.
jokes.punchline: 'Clots in his legs' rushing to his brain — both medically plausible and used as an insult against the Speaker. 'Biting James Bond's cable car wire' as a Jonah insult — referencing a Jaws-style image of Jonah's large size.
jokes.explanation: The 'clots rushing to the brain' medical detail is dark-funny. The Bond cable car image is vivid and specific — Furlong's insults always have cinematic reference points. Good character integration.

jokes.text: if we don't raise the debt ceiling, America's financial system is gonna go belly-up like what, Will? — Like my labradoodle, Teddy, when I cover his cock in honey and give him a sticky licky.
jokes.setup: Furlong is explaining the debt ceiling consequences and asks Will to complete the analogy.
jokes.punchline: Will's 'sticky licky' — a horrifying image involving his own dog's genitals — is so far from any reasonable completion of the analogy that it becomes pure Veep absurdism. Will's inability to read a room is perfect.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The setup (a serious political analogy being delegated to Will) primes for a straight answer. Will's response ('sticky licky') is maximally wrong in every dimension — wrong content, wrong register, wrong audience. 'Teddy' being named makes it worse. 'A sticky licky' as the exact phrase used is inspired. Furlong's '— No, bad!' response elevates it further. Justifies 8.5+ through originality and the complete comedic surprise of the misdirection.

jokes.text: Well, maybe the government needs to be shut down because it's broken. And when something's broken, you shut it down, and then you turn it back on again like with a router.
jokes.setup: Jonah is defending his decision to allow a government shutdown.
jokes.punchline: The router analogy — applied to the entire United States government — as if government is a piece of home networking equipment that just needs to be restarted.
jokes.explanation: The router analogy is genuinely brilliant in its stupidity. It reflects a real type of tech-bro thinking applied to governance, which makes it both absurd and terrifyingly plausible. Only Jonah would use this analogy with full confidence. Highly quotable and original. Justifies 8+ through the quality of the underlying satirical observation wrapped in Jonah's specific form of stupidity.

jokes.text: And if they didn't want me to shut down the government, then maybe they should've invited me to the Meyer unveiling!
jokes.setup: Jonah is debating the constitutional necessity of a government shutdown.
jokes.punchline: The 'real' reason for the shutdown is revealed: not principle, but a party invitation. The entire financial crisis was triggered by a social snub.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's structural punchline — the entire B-plot's payoff. The joke works because the show has earned it: we've watched Jonah's obsession with the invitation grow from petty grievance to nuclear-level policy sabotage. The revelation that a government shutdown — with all its economic consequences — traces back to a party invitation not received is satirical perfection about political ego. Character integration is absolute maximum: only Jonah could be the proximate cause of economic crisis via social slight. One of the episode's two best jokes.

jokes.text: And if they didn't want me to shut down the government, then maybe they should've invited me to the Meyer unveiling!
jokes.setup: Jonah is explaining his principled stand on the government shutdown.
jokes.punchline: The actual motivation for the shutdown is revealed: Jonah wasn't invited to a party. A national economic crisis motivated by a hurt feelings over a guest list.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best structural payoffs. The entire Jonah subplot has been about not being invited to the party; the government shutdown is the escalated consequence. The comedic logic (a national crisis caused by a petty slight) is executed with perfect economy — Jonah states it plainly, as a reasonable position. This is Veep's satirical thesis in one sentence: government dysfunction is personal pettiness at scale. Justifies 8.9 through structure, character integration, and the thematic weight the joke carries.

jokes.text: Look at this place. It is such a dump, isn't it? — Although, I have to admit I sort of miss it. — Aw, and it misses you, too. The one who got away. — And now she's back for her big day!
jokes.setup: Selina arrives at the White House for the portrait unveiling.
jokes.punchline: Gary immediately anthropomorphizes the White House as a lover who misses Selina — 'The one who got away' and 'back for her big day' — treating the building as a romantic partner who pined for her.
jokes.explanation: Gary's devotion extending to anthropomorphizing the White House on Selina's behalf is consistent character comedy. 'The one who got away' is a nice romantic framing for a building.

jokes.text: Are you wearing makeup? — No. — Yeah.
jokes.setup: Selina notices something different about Gary at the White House.
jokes.punchline: Gary denies wearing makeup; Selina's flat 'Yeah.' confirms she knows he's lying — and implies Gary wearing makeup for the occasion is entirely plausible.
jokes.explanation: Three words of comedy. Gary's denial is the setup; Selina's single-syllable 'Yeah' is the punchline. The inference that Gary has clearly dressed up for the occasion, and that this is funny but not surprising, is carried entirely by context. Maximum economy.

jokes.text: I have no idea what these people's names are. — I know one thing... she's the one who put my sweater in the dryer.
jokes.setup: White House staff greet Selina warmly, using her name. She responds warmly in kind.
jokes.punchline: Selina whispers to Gary that she doesn't know anyone's name — she recognizes one woman only because of a sweater-ruining incident, not because she's a person.
jokes.explanation: The sweater detail is specific enough to be hilarious — Selina's only concrete memory of a staff member is a laundry incident. This is excellent character comedy: she can remember a sweater injustice but not a human being.

jokes.text: What is this Dixie cup shit show? It's like we teleported to post-Katrina Mississippi.
jokes.setup: Selina arrives at her designated room and finds minimal preparations — chips, Coke Zero, Dixie cups.
jokes.punchline: 'Post-Katrina Mississippi' as the reference point for inadequate party preparation — a geographically and historically specific comparison that reveals both Selina's standards and her casual contempt.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'post-Katrina Mississippi' for a complaint about cups is both funny and revealing — Selina's reference bank for deprivation is a major national disaster. Character-specific and vivid.

jokes.text: Montez is really taking this Mexican thing too far.
jokes.setup: Selina is complaining about the party setup.
jokes.punchline: Blaming the austerity on President Montez's ethnicity — 'the Mexican thing' — as if Montez is intentionally catering to Mexican minimalism, revealing Selina's reflexive racism.
jokes.explanation: Consistent with Selina's casual racism established in the opening scene. 'The Mexican thing' as a catch-all explanation for government austerity is darkly funny.

jokes.text: Jonah and his merry band of jizz huffers drove their short bus right into the debt ceiling vote and T-boned the entire US economy.
jokes.setup: Amy reports what has happened with the government shutdown.
jokes.punchline: 'Merry band of jizz huffers' and 'short bus' — Amy's insult vocabulary stepping up to describe the Jeffersons. The transportation metaphors (short bus → T-boned) are consistent.
jokes.explanation: 'Merry band of jizz huffers' is Amy at her most creative — the 'merry band' framing is classically ironic against the content. The short bus → T-boned construction sustains the metaphor with commitment. Justifies 8+ through originality and the sustained metaphorical consistency.

jokes.text: We just hit the 12-week mark. It's a boy. [REACTION BEAT — the room processes the gender reveal after all the gender politics discussion]
jokes.setup: Earlier in the episode, Catherine refused to reveal the baby's sex, leading to an extensive gender politics argument.
jokes.punchline: Catherine now casually reveals 'It's a boy' in the middle of a government shutdown crisis — the simplest possible answer, delivered with no ceremony, after all the earlier philosophical turmoil.
jokes.explanation: The callback payoff is elegant — after pages of gender politics debate, Catherine drops 'It's a boy' in the most anticlimactic context possible, during a national emergency. The simplicity of the revelation after all that complication is the joke. Perfect structural payoff. Justifies 8.5 through structure and the economy of the callback.

jokes.text: Congressman Furlong — Brookheimer! Got your dong of a résumé. I know you'd chew your own arm off to escape being handcuffed to Meyers's overly toned corpse and her dungeon of losers, but I had to go in another direction. Tell her why, Will.
jokes.setup: Amy has apparently applied to work for Furlong. He's rejecting her.
jokes.punchline: 'Overly toned corpse' for Selina; 'dungeon of losers' for the team; 'dong of a résumé' for Amy's qualifications — three separate insults in rapid succession, then delegating the rejection explanation to Will.
jokes.explanation: Furlong's insult density at full power. 'Overly toned corpse' for Selina is inspired — acknowledging her fitness while calling her dead. 'Dungeon of losers' is vivid. The delegation to Will for explanation is classic Furlong structure. Justifies 8+ through the density and quality of the insults.

jokes.text: You're stronger than me, and you have a higher reserve of dignity and self-worth. And I'm trapped in a cycle of abuse. Better luck next time.
jokes.setup: Furlong has asked Will to explain why Amy didn't get the job.
jokes.punchline: Will's explanation is entirely self-incriminating — he describes himself as weaker, with less dignity, trapped in an abusive dynamic — while cheerfully delivering the rejection. 'Better luck next time' as a button.
jokes.explanation: Will's self-awareness about his own abuse (while remaining in the situation) is one of the show's recurring great jokes, but this execution is exceptional — he articulates Amy's superiority as the reason she wasn't hired, while admitting his own diminishment. The structure of delivering rejection through self-condemnation is original and character-perfect. Justifies 8.5+ through the quality of Will's character integration.

jokes.text: Hey, Fuckleberry Finn! Christ, you got old.
jokes.setup: Furlong spots someone else (presumably Tom James or another character) across the room.
jokes.punchline: 'Fuckleberry Finn' as a greeting, then 'Christ, you got old' — the insult is the greeting.
jokes.explanation: 'Fuckleberry Finn' is a good Furlong portmanteau insult — the literary reference slightly elevating the profanity. 'Christ, you got old' as a greeting is very Furlong.

jokes.text: You wanna work for that guy? — I really do.
jokes.setup: Selina has witnessed Furlong's behavior toward Amy.
jokes.punchline: Amy's genuine, unambiguous 'I really do' — she prefers Furlong's obvious abuse to working for Selina, which is a devastating assessment of both.
jokes.explanation: Three words. Amy's absolute sincerity in preferring Furlong's open hostility to Selina's chaotic dysfunction is a brilliant character moment. The economy is exceptional — 'I really do' carries enormous weight about both characters. Justifies 8+ through economy and character integration.

jokes.text: Can I have a private word with you? — Would that be all right, or are you afraid that I'll rape you again?
jokes.setup: Selina spots Tom James at the unveiling and wants to talk privately.
jokes.punchline: 'Or are you afraid I'll rape you again?' — addressing the TV interview accusation directly, treating it with Selina's characteristic aggression rather than shame.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the rape accusation, now weaponized by Selina in person, is a good structural payoff. Her willingness to invoke it casually and publicly shows both her aggression and her tactical deployment of shame as a weapon.

jokes.text: I came to you in good faith and I asked! — Well... — I did the classy thing. — Come on, we both know you were gonna go ahead and do it anyway. — Of course I was, but I had the courtesy to seem like I might not!
jokes.setup: Selina confronts Tom about going public with the affair after she'd politely consulted him.
jokes.punchline: 'Of course I was, but I had the courtesy to seem like I might not!' — Selina's concept of ethical behavior is theater, not substance. She's furious he went public, not that she was going to do the same.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best and most quotable lines — and one of the show's best encapsulations of Selina's character. Her moral framework is entirely about appearances and process, not substance. 'I had the courtesy to seem like I might not' is brilliant because it's genuinely how Washington works — the performance of integrity, not integrity itself. This competes with the 9+ anchor examples. Justifies the 9 through the depth of the character insight, the originality of the comedic concept, and near-perfect economy.

jokes.text: You've got more issues than 'National Geographic.'
jokes.setup: Tom James is responding to Selina's aggressive confrontation.
jokes.punchline: 'More issues than National Geographic' — a pun on 'issues' (personal problems / magazine issues), using a publication famous for its longevity.
jokes.explanation: A solid pun — 'issues' doing double duty as psychological problems and magazine volumes. The National Geographic specificity (a publication that has been publishing since 1888) adds to the implied quantity. Clean, quotable, but not the most original construction.

jokes.text: Oh, oh, poor Tommy. He wanted me to call him afterwards. — Shut the fuck up!
jokes.setup: Selina has just extracted an accidental admission from Tom that he would have wanted her to call after their encounter.
jokes.punchline: She immediately broadcasts it — 'Poor Tommy. He wanted me to call him afterwards.' — to whoever is within earshot, then Tom's furious 'Shut the fuck up!' confirms it.
jokes.explanation: Selina's cruelty in publicly humiliating Tom with his own emotional vulnerability is very well-executed. 'Poor Tommy' in the third person while he's standing there is a specific comedic choice that maximizes the indignity. His furious denial-confirmation tops it perfectly.

jokes.text: Madam President, thank you for inviting us to your unveiling. — My wife is a huge fan. [beat — Tom James's wife appears]
jokes.setup: Tom has just been publicly humiliated by Selina about wanting a post-sex phone call. Someone approaches.
jokes.punchline: Tom James introduces his wife — who has clearly just witnessed some of this — and adds 'My wife is a huge fan,' which given the context is maximally awkward.
jokes.explanation: The structural cringe of the wife's arrival at the precise worst moment is classic Veep timing. 'My wife is a huge fan' given everything that just happened is wonderfully uncomfortable.

jokes.text: Yeah, well, you know what, you can have your trophy wife and your trophy office and your trophy baby, who you won't even live to see go to college, but I still got to be president, and, tsk, you never will.
jokes.setup: Selina has privately acknowledged she still has feelings for Tom. She recovers by constructing a victory speech.
jokes.punchline: The 'trophy baby, who you won't even live to see go to college' — predicting Tom's death before the child graduates — is a startling escalation. The final 'you never will' about the presidency is her ultimate weapon.
jokes.explanation: The trophy wife/office/baby escalation builds well, then the death prediction ('won't even live to see go to college') catches by surprise — it's not just a put-down, it's Selina wishing Tom James dead. The final 'tsk, you never will' about the presidency is cold and devastating, and because it's delivered internally (to herself), it has a lonely quality. One of the episode's best Selina moments.

jokes.text: you can have your trophy wife and your trophy office and your trophy baby, who you won't even live to see go to college, but I still got to be president, and, tsk, you never will.
jokes.setup: Selina has confronted Tom James extensively. She delivers a final parting shot.
jokes.punchline: 'Who you won't even live to see go to college' — a casual and specific death threat/prediction about Tom's age. Then the presidential trump card: 'I still got to be president, and you never will.'
jokes.explanation: The 'trophy' accumulation (wife/office/baby) sets up for a reversal, and 'who you won't even live to see go to college' is a startling dark pivot — she's predicting his death based on his age. The presidential trump card at the end ('I still got to be president') is Selina's ultimate weapon and she knows it. Strong structure and character integration.

jokes.text: I'm back. What the hell was that? — You really wanted me, didn't you? — There were sparks practically flying out of your dick!
jokes.setup: Selina, having announced she swears Tom wanted her, goes BACK to Tom to tell him.
jokes.punchline: She confronts him directly: 'You really wanted me, didn't you? There were sparks practically flying out of your dick!'
jokes.explanation: 'Sparks practically flying out of your dick' is an extraordinary phrase — it applies electrical attraction metaphor to anatomy with complete specificity. Selina returning to re-engage Tom after just walking away is perfect behavioral comedy — she cannot let it go. The escalation of her needing to be right about this is wonderful.

jokes.text: No, no, I'm not talking about the Green Room. That was just a good, old-fashioned hate fuck.
jokes.setup: Selina is telling Tom James she senses chemistry between them NOW, in the present.
jokes.punchline: 'That was just a good, old-fashioned hate fuck' — dismissing the original encounter as genre-categorically different from what she's sensing now, as if 'hate fuck' is a neutral descriptive term.
jokes.explanation: 'Good, old-fashioned hate fuck' as a taxonomy that requires no further explanation from Selina's perspective is very funny. The 'old-fashioned' modifier is the best word — treating it as a respectable tradition.

jokes.text: You want to throw me onto this couch and pin my legs behind my head and do me. — I am a happily married man, Selina. — Oh, that's not an answer!
jokes.setup: Selina is escalating the confrontation with Tom James, accusing him of wanting her sexually.
jokes.punchline: 'I am a happily married man' as the deflection — and Selina's devastating 'Oh, that's not an answer!' — because she's right, it isn't an answer to the specific question.
jokes.explanation: 'That's not an answer' is both legally/rhetorically correct (marriage status doesn't preclude desire) and completely unhinged in context. Selina using courtroom logic on sexual desire is a perfect character moment.

jokes.text: Alethia? Is that her name or is that the pill you take to fuck her?
jokes.setup: Tom James introduces his wife Alethia to Selina.
jokes.punchline: 'Is that her name or is that the pill you take to fuck her?' — treating 'Alethia' as sounding like a pharmaceutical drug name, implying Tom needs chemical assistance.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest jokes. The pharmaceutical comparison works on multiple levels: (1) Alethia does sound like a medication name, (2) it implies Tom is sexually dysfunctional with his wife, (3) it's delivered directly to Alethia herself, (4) Selina clearly doesn't care who hears it. The structure is flawless — the question framing is perfectly calibrated. The delivery in front of the wife maximizes the cringe. This competes with the episode's best moments for quotability. Justifies 9 through originality, structure, economy, and the audacity of the target and context.

jokes.text: I voted for you. — Well, it didn't help.
jokes.setup: Alethia tells Selina she voted for her.
jokes.punchline: 'Well, it didn't help.' — Selina's flat acknowledgment that the vote was useless, which is both self-deprecating and dismissive simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: Four words. Maximum economy. 'Well, it didn't help' is simultaneously self-aware, bitter, dismissive of Alethia's support, and a perfect encapsulation of Selina's relationship with her own failure. The fact that she's thanking a supporter by noting their vote was wasted is exquisitely Selina. Justifies 8.5+ through economy and the depth of character revealed in one phrase.

jokes.text: that when I was in the loony bin, I thought about you endlessly — Tom? — Sweetheart. This is my wife, Alethia.
jokes.setup: Selina has escalated into an emotional confession — she thought about Tom in the 'loony bin.' She's fully vulnerable.
jokes.punchline: Tom's interruption reveals his wife has been standing there for the entire confession. 'Sweetheart. This is my wife, Alethia.' — timing the introduction to maximally humiliate Selina.
jokes.explanation: The structural cringe payoff is perfectly executed. Selina has gone fully vulnerable — mentioning a psychiatric stay and romantic pining — and the wife has been present for it. The comedy of the timing is the joke; the humiliation is complete. Justifies 8.5 through structural excellence and the maximum-cringe deployment.

jokes.text: Oh, wow, she looks awesome. I'd give my left tit for those tits.
jokes.setup: President Montez enters the room looking impressive.
jokes.punchline: Selina's assessment — 'I'd give my left tit for those tits' — is an extremely crude body-envy comment about her successor delivered under her breath.
jokes.explanation: The phrase works through its construction — 'give my left tit for those tits' is both a body-specific variant on 'give my right arm' and a crude objectification of Montez. Selina's immediate pivot from admiring Montez to envying her body is consistent character comedy.

jokes.text: There are so many things that I would like to say about the extraordinary woman who was my predecessor here at the White House. — Here comes the love! — Shh, I know. Shh. — But unfortunately, I have to return to the Oval Office to deal with the shutdown.
jokes.setup: Montez begins what sounds like a glowing tribute speech for Selina's portrait unveiling. Gary is excited.
jokes.punchline: Montez immediately pivots to leaving because of the government shutdown — canceling the speech entirely. The tribute Selina has been anticipating all episode is aborted in two sentences.
jokes.explanation: The episode's structural payoff. The speech that opened the episode ('that just tickles my twat') is the one Montez abandons in front of everyone. The irony — a government shutdown caused by Jonah not being invited to the unveiling cancels the unveiling speech — closes the satirical loop perfectly. Gary's 'Here comes the love!' anticipation is the perfect setup for the abandonment. Justifies 8.5+ through structural elegance and the payoff of the entire episode's premise.

jokes.text: I look around this room and I see so many portraits of... of so many of our great presidents... But I also like to think perhaps about their not-so-shining moments. And that, um... well, maybe none of it matters.
jokes.setup: With Montez gone, Selina must give an impromptu speech at her own portrait unveiling, having prepared nothing.
jokes.punchline: She ends up in existential nihilism — 'maybe none of it matters' — as the climax of what should be a triumphant legacy speech. Delivered sincerely, in real time.
jokes.explanation: The existential drift of Selina's speech — reaching 'maybe none of it matters' at a portrait unveiling — is darkly funny and thematically rich. It's a genuinely good dramatic moment that's also comedic because of the context and the complete absence of a prepared speech.

jokes.text: So, out of respect for the furlough, um, I think we should wrap things up. So, thank you so much. What a... what a wonderful thing. Thank you.
jokes.setup: Selina ends her portrait unveiling speech with a whimper.
jokes.punchline: 'Out of respect for the furlough' — treating the government shutdown as a polite excuse to stop speaking, as if it's a social nicety. 'What a... what a wonderful thing' with its stumbling is the understated comedy.
jokes.explanation: 'Out of respect for the furlough' is a nice absurdist corporate-speak application. The stumbling sign-off is appropriately anticlimactic.

jokes.text: Yeah, that cow put the chair in the painting. — What chair? — The fucking chair in that painting is the chair that she made love to Andrew on. — I slept in that chair.
jokes.setup: After the unveiling, Selina notices something wrong with her portrait.
jokes.punchline: The chair in the official presidential portrait is where she had sex with Andrew. Mike reveals he slept in it — completing a disgusting circle of intimate furniture use.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'sex chair' to 'Mike slept in it' to 'it's now immortalized in an official portrait' is a great three-beat comedy structure. Mike's 'I slept in that chair' delivered without any realization of the implication is perfect. The chair in the national portrait as a monument to indignity is a strong visual/comedic concept.

jokes.text: Yeah, that cow put the chair in the painting. — What chair? — The fucking chair in that painting is the chair that she made love to Andrew on. — I slept in that chair.
jokes.setup: After the portrait is unveiled, Selina and Gary study it and discover something.
jokes.punchline: The chair in Selina's official portrait is the chair on which Selina's ex-husband (Andrew) had an affair. Gary knows because he slept in it.
jokes.explanation: Structurally excellent. Three revelations: 1) The painter included a specific chair. 2) That chair is where Andrew cheated on Selina. 3) Gary slept in it — confirming both the affair's casual acceptance in the household and Gary's devotion (he literally slept near where her husband had sex). 'That cow put the chair in the painting' as the opener sets up perfectly. The escalation from 'there's a chair' to 'Gary slept in it' is masterful.

jokes.text: And look at my neck. I... no, in the painting! Look at my neck. I have pardoned turkeys with fewer waddles.
jokes.setup: Selina continues examining her portrait and finds another grievance.
jokes.punchline: 'I have pardoned turkeys with fewer waddles' — the comparison of her neck in the painting to a turkey neck, using the specific presidential action of turkey pardoning as the frame.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional line. 'Pardoned turkeys with fewer waddles' is the kind of joke that only works in this specific show — it requires the character to be a president, and it uses a specifically presidential action (turkey pardoning) as the measuring tool for her neck's turkey-ness. The joke is self-deprecating but also narcissistic (she's this concerned about a painted neck). One of the episode's most quotable lines.

jokes.text: And look at my neck. I... no, in the painting! Look at my neck. I have pardoned turkeys with fewer waddles.
jokes.setup: Selina is examining her official portrait.
jokes.punchline: 'I have pardoned turkeys with fewer waddles' — comparing her portrait neck to a turkey's, with the specific detail that she has physically seen these turkeys and they look better.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable lines. The turkey pardoning is a real presidential tradition; specifying that the pardoned turkeys have better necks than her portrait is an inspired specific comparison. The setup (Gary misunderstanding which neck she means) and the clarification ('no, in the painting!') add a layer. 'Fewer waddles' as the metric is perfect. Justifies 8.5+ through originality, economy, and very high quotability.

jokes.text: Not exactly. I have a law degree and served two tours in Afghanistan. — Oh, congrats.
jokes.setup: John Corbin is introduced as 'Montez's me' (i.e., her version of Gary). He corrects the implication.
jokes.punchline: He lists his impressive credentials (law degree, military service). Selina's response: 'Oh, congrats.' — total indifference to accomplishments that bear no relation to her needs.
jokes.explanation: 'Oh, congrats.' delivered to a veteran/lawyer who has just listed their qualifications, by Selina who is comprehensively uninterested, is a perfect deadpan. The economy of the dismissal is excellent.

jokes.text: Ma'am, the painting is stunning. — Well, thank you, Marjorie, but you're not exactly known for your good taste.
jokes.setup: Marjorie compliments the portrait.
jokes.punchline: Selina accepts the compliment and immediately disqualifies the complimenter — Marjorie's approval is actually a warning sign given her taste.
jokes.explanation: Good structure — accepting a compliment while invalidating it is a Veep specialty. The irony that Marjorie's taste (Catherine, vegetarianism, presumably) is objectively niche makes this land.

jokes.text: Jesus Christ, underage Muslim brides are less traumatized at their unveiling. Shh.
jokes.setup: Selina is complaining about the humiliations of her own portrait unveiling.
